data.title: Eleco's (LON:ELCO) three-year earnings growth trails the 28% YoY shareholder returns
data.description:
The most you can lose on any stock (assuming you don't use leverage) is 100% of your money. But when you pick a company that is really flourishing, you can make more than 100%. For instance the Eleco plc (LON:ELCO) share price is 101% higher than it was three years ago. That sort of return is as solid as granite. And in the last week the share price has popped 16%.

Since it's been a strong week for Eleco shareholders, let's have a look at trend of the longer term fundamentals.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

To paraphrase Benjamin Graham: Over the short term the market is a voting machine, but over the long term it's a weighing machine. By comparing earnings per share (EPS) and share price changes over time, we can get a feel for how investor attitudes to a company have morphed over time.

Eleco was able to grow its EPS at 17% per year over three years, sending the share price higher. In comparison, the 26% per year gain in the share price outpaces the EPS growth. This suggests that, as the business progressed over the last few years, it gained the confidence of market participants. It's not unusual to see the market 're-rate' a stock, after a few years of growth.

You can see below how EPS has changed over time (discover the exact values by clicking on the image).

We consider it positive that insiders have made significant purchases in the last year. Having said that, most people consider earnings and revenue growth trends to be a more meaningful guide to the business. This free interactive report on Eleco's earnings, revenue and cash flow is a great place to start, if you want to investigate the stock further.

Advertisement

What About Dividends?

When looking at investment returns, it is important to consider the difference between total shareholder return (TSR) and share price return. The TSR is a return calculation that accounts for the value of cash dividends (assuming that any dividend received was reinvested) and the calculated value of any discounted capital raisings and spin-offs. So for companies that pay a generous dividend, the TSR is often a lot higher than the share price return. In the case of Eleco, it has a TSR of 107% for the last 3 years. That exceeds its share price return that we previously mentioned. And there's no prize for guessing that the dividend payments largely explain the divergence!

A Different Perspective

Eleco provided a TSR of 2.0% over the last twelve months. But that was short of the market average. It's probably a good sign that the company has an even better long term track record, having provided shareholders with an annual TSR of 10% over five years. Maybe the share price is just taking a breather while the business executes on its growth strategy. If you want to research this stock further, the data on insider buying is an obvious place to start. You can click here to see who has been buying shares - and the price they paid.

Eleco is not the only stock insiders are buying. So take a peek at this free list of small cap companies at attractive valuations which insiders have been buying.

Please note, the market returns quoted in this article reflect the market weighted average returns of stocks that currently trade on British exchanges.

data.title: Calculating The Intrinsic Value Of Goodwin PLC (LON:GDWN)
data.description:
Advertisement

Key Insights

  • Goodwin's estimated fair value is UK£314 based on 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity
  • Current share price of UK£261 suggests Goodwin is potentially trading close to its fair value
  • Peers of Goodwin are currently trading on average at a 19% premium

How far off is Goodwin PLC (LON:GDWN) from its intrinsic value? Using the most recent financial data, we'll take a look at whether the stock is fairly priced by taking the forecast future cash flows of the company and discounting them back to today's value. One way to achieve this is by employing the Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model. Before you think you won't be able to understand it, just read on! It's actually much less complex than you'd imagine.

We would caution that there are many ways of valuing a company and, like the DCF, each technique has advantages and disadvantages in certain scenarios. Anyone interested in learning a bit more about intrinsic value should have a read of the Simply Wall St analysis model.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Crunching The Numbers

We are going to use a two-stage DCF model, which, as the name states, takes into account two stages of growth. The first stage is generally a higher growth period which levels off heading towards the terminal value, captured in the second 'steady growth' period. To begin with, we have to get estimates of the next ten years of cash flows. Seeing as no analyst estimates of free cash flow are available to us, we have extrapolate the previous free cash flow (FCF) from the company's last reported value. We assume companies with shrinking free cash flow will slow their rate of shrinkage, and that companies with growing free cash flow will see their growth rate slow, over this period. We do this to reflect that growth tends to slow more in the early years than it does in later years.

A DCF is all about the idea that a dollar in the future is less valuable than a dollar today, so we discount the value of these future cash flows to their estimated value in today's dollars:

10-year free cash flow (FCF) estimate

2026202720282029203020312032203320342035
Levered FCF (£, Millions) UK£67.9mUK£87.6mUK£106.2mUK£122.9mUK£137.6mUK£150.3mUK£161.3mUK£171.1mUK£179.9mUK£187.9m
Growth Rate Estimate SourceEst @ 40.15%Est @ 29.00%Est @ 21.20%Est @ 15.73%Est @ 11.91%Est @ 9.23%Est @ 7.36%Est @ 6.05%Est @ 5.13%Est @ 4.49%
Present Value (£, Millions) Discounted @ 8.6% UK£62.6UK£74.3UK£83.0UK£88.4UK£91.1UK£91.7UK£90.7UK£88.5UK£85.7UK£82.5

("Est" = FCF growth rate estimated by Simply Wall St)
Present Value of 10-year Cash Flow (PVCF) = UK£839m

The second stage is also known as Terminal Value, this is the business's cash flow after the first stage. For a number of reasons a very conservative growth rate is used that cannot exceed that of a country's GDP growth. In this case we have used the 5-year average of the 10-year government bond yield (3.0%) to estimate future growth. In the same way as with the 10-year 'growth' period, we discount future cash flows to today's value, using a cost of equity of 8.6%.

Terminal Value (TV)= FCF2035 × (1 + g) ÷ (r – g) = UK£188m× (1 + 3.0%) ÷ (8.6%– 3.0%) = UK£3.5b

Present Value of Terminal Value (PVTV)= TV / (1 + r)10= UK£3.5b÷ ( 1 + 8.6%)10= UK£1.5b

The total value, or equity value, is then the sum of the present value of the future cash flows, which in this case is UK£2.4b. To get the intrinsic value per share, we divide this by the total number of shares outstanding. Relative to the current share price of UK£261, the company appears about fair value at a 17% discount to where the stock price trades currently. The assumptions in any calculation have a big impact on the valuation, so it is better to view this as a rough estimate, not precise down to the last cent.

The Assumptions

Now the most important inputs to a discounted cash flow are the discount rate, and of course, the actual cash flows. If you don't agree with these result, have a go at the calculation yourself and play with the assumptions. The DCF also does not consider the possible cyclicality of an industry, or a company's future capital requirements, so it does not give a full picture of a company's potential performance. Given that we are looking at Goodwin as potential shareholders, the cost of equity is used as the discount rate, rather than the cost of capital (or weighted average cost of capital, WACC) which accounts for debt. In this calculation we've used 8.6%, which is based on a levered beta of 1.096. Beta is a measure of a stock's volatility, compared to the market as a whole. We get our beta from the industry average beta of globally comparable companies, with an imposed limit between 0.8 and 2.0, which is a reasonable range for a stable business.

View our latest analysis for Goodwin

SWOT Analysis for Goodwin

Strength
  • Earnings growth over the past year exceeded the industry.
  • Debt is not viewed as a risk.
    Balance sheet summary for GDWN.
Weakness
  • Dividend is low compared to the top 25% of dividend payers in the Machinery market.
    Key risks with investing in GDWN.
Opportunity
  • Current share price is below our estimate of fair value.
  • Lack of analyst coverage makes it difficult to determine GDWN's earnings prospects.
Threat
  • No apparent threats visible for GDWN.

Next Steps:

Valuation is only one side of the coin in terms of building your investment thesis, and it ideally won't be the sole piece of analysis you scrutinize for a company. It's not possible to obtain a foolproof valuation with a DCF model. Instead the best use for a DCF model is to test certain assumptions and theories to see if they would lead to the company being undervalued or overvalued. For instance, if the terminal value growth rate is adjusted slightly, it can dramatically alter the overall result. For Goodwin, we've compiled three relevant items you should consider:

  1. Risks: Consider for instance, the ever-present spectre of investment risk. We've identified 2 warning signs with Goodwin , and understanding these should be part of your investment process.
  2. Management:Have insiders been ramping up their shares to take advantage of the market's sentiment for GDWN's future outlook? Check out our management and board analysis with insights on CEO compensation and governance factors.
  3. Other High Quality Alternatives: Do you like a good all-rounder? Explore our interactive list of high quality stocks to get an idea of what else is out there you may be missing!

data.title: Chemung Financial (CHMG) Margin Compression Versus 24.3% Prior-Year Net Margin Tests Bullish Narratives
data.description:
Chemung Financial (CHMG) has just wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter total revenue of US$29.4 million and basic EPS of US$1.61, alongside net income excluding extra items of US$7.7 million. Over the past few quarters, the company has seen total revenue range from US$24.6 million to US$29.4 million while basic EPS moved between US$1.24 in Q4 2024 and US$1.61 in Q4 2025, with a sharp loss in Q2 2025 breaking that pattern. Taken together with the latest print, the focus is on how sustainably Chemung Financial can manage its margins from here.

See our full analysis for Chemung Financial.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set these results against the widely followed narratives about Chemung Financial to see which views the data supports and which ones are challenged by the reported figures.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Margins and Costs Move Together

  • Across 2025, Chemung Financial's reported net interest margin ranged from 2.96% in Q1 to 3.45% in Q3, while the cost to income ratio moved between 61.18% and 65.69% over the same quarters.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is that margin and cost metrics in 2025, such as the 3.45% net interest margin and 61.18% cost to income ratio in Q3, line up with the idea of a traditional community bank that relies on spread income and disciplined expense control. However, the trailing net profit margin of 16.7% versus 24.3% a year earlier shows that recent profitability is not as strong as that optimistic framing suggests.
    • Bulls who like the long history and traditional model still have to reconcile that lower 16.7% trailing margin with earlier levels near 24.3% cited in the data.
    • At the same time, basic EPS in the individual 2025 quarters ranged from a loss of US$1.34 in Q2 to profits of US$1.26 to US$1.62 in the other quarters, which shows that the bank's earnings pattern has been uneven even within a single year.

Loan Book Growth with Mixed Credit Signals

  • Total loans in the periodical data stepped from US$2,028.95 million in Q3 2024 to US$2,202.36 million in Q3 2025, while non performing loans moved in a band between US$7.76 million and US$10.55 million across the reported quarters.
  • Critics who take a more bearish stance may focus on the fact that non performing loans sat at US$10.55 million in Q3 2024 and US$9.88 million in Q1 2025, even as total loans increased. This means the growing loan book and the exposure figures given in the data leave room for bears to argue that asset quality needs to be watched closely.
    • The combination of higher total loans of over US$2.2b and non performing loans still around US$7.76 million to US$9.88 million in several quarters keeps credit costs in the conversation for cautious investors.
    • Because net interest margin in those same quarters was between 2.72% and 3.45%, any shift in non performing loans within that US$7.76 million to US$10.55 million range could have a direct impact on future profitability metrics that bears are likely to track closely.

Valuation Gap Between P/E and DCF Fair Value

  • The stock trades on a trailing P/E of 18.8x with a share price of US$59.11, while the supplied DCF fair value is US$133.07 and the cited analyst price target is US$64.25, and the modelled comparison suggests the market price is about 55.6% below that DCF fair value.
  • What is relevant for a bullish angle is that forecasts in the dataset point to earnings growth of about 38.6% per year and revenue growth of about 19% per year, alongside a 2.3% dividend yield. However, the trailing P/E of 18.8x is higher than both the 12.4x peer average and the 11.8x US Banks industry, so the case for upside rests on those growth forecasts and the DCF fair value of US$133.07 being more important than the premium multiple on past earnings.
    • Supporters of the stock can point to trailing 12 month net income of US$15.10 million on revenue of US$90.67 million and the 16.7% profit margin as the earnings base that the 38.6% forecast growth rate is applied to.
    • Skeptics, on the other hand, can counter that the same P/E of 18.8x, which is above both peer and industry averages, already prices in some of that forecast growth even before any change in the DCF fair value or the US$64.25 price target is reflected in the share price of US$59.11.

data.title: While shareholders of Future (LON:FUTR) are in the red over the last five years, underlying earnings have actually grown
data.description:
It's nice to see the Future plc (LON:FUTR) share price up 11% in a week. But that is little comfort to those holding over the last half decade, sitting on a big loss. In fact, the share price has declined rather badly, down some 70% in that time. Some might say the recent bounce is to be expected after such a bad drop. But it could be that the fall was overdone.

The recent uptick of 11% could be a positive sign of things to come, so let's take a look at historical fundamentals.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

In his essay The Superinvestors of Graham-and-Doddsville Warren Buffett described how share prices do not always rationally reflect the value of a business. One imperfect but simple way to consider how the market perception of a company has shifted is to compare the change in the earnings per share (EPS) with the share price movement.

While the share price declined over five years, Future actually managed to increase EPS by an average of 8.9% per year. Given the share price reaction, one might suspect that EPS is not a good guide to the business performance during the period (perhaps due to a one-off loss or gain). Or possibly, the market was previously very optimistic, so the stock has disappointed, despite improving EPS.

Because of the sharp contrast between the EPS growth rate and the share price growth, we're inclined to look to other metrics to understand the changing market sentiment around the stock.

In contrast to the share price, revenue has actually increased by 9.6% a year in the five year period. A more detailed examination of the revenue and earnings may or may not explain why the share price languishes; there could be an opportunity.

A Different Perspective

Investors in Future had a tough year, with a total loss of 41% (including dividends), against a market gain of about 26%. Even the share prices of good stocks drop sometimes, but we want to see improvements in the fundamental metrics of a business, before getting too interested. Regrettably, last year's performance caps off a bad run, with the shareholders facing a total loss of 11% per year over five years. Generally speaking long term share price weakness can be a bad sign, though contrarian investors might want to research the stock in hope of a turnaround. data.title: With EPS Growth And More, REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS (ELI:RENE) Makes An Interesting Case
data.description:
Investors are often guided by the idea of discovering 'the next big thing', even if that means buying 'story stocks' without any revenue, let alone profit. But the reality is that when a company loses money each year, for long enough, its investors will usually take their share of those losses. A loss-making company is yet to prove itself with profit, and eventually the inflow of external capital may dry up.

So if this idea of high risk and high reward doesn't suit, you might be more interested in profitable, growing companies, like REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS (ELI:RENE). Now this is not to say that the company presents the best investment opportunity around, but profitability is a key component to success in business.

How Fast Is REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS Growing?

The market is a voting machine in the short term, but a weighing machine in the long term, so you'd expect share price to follow earnings per share (EPS) outcomes eventually. So it makes sense that experienced investors pay close attention to company EPS when undertaking investment research. We can see that in the last three years REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS grew its EPS by 16% per year. That's a pretty good rate, if the company can sustain it.

Careful consideration of revenue growth and earnings before interest and taxation (EBIT) margins can help inform a view on the sustainability of the recent profit growth. On the revenue front, REN - Redes Energéticas Nacionais SGPS has done well over the past year, growing revenue by 8.2% to €1.0b but EBIT margin figures were less stellar, seeing a decline over the last 12 months. Prior to investment, it's always a good idea to check that the management team is paid reasonably. Pay levels around or below the median, can be a sign that shareholder interests are well considered. data.title: Park National (PRK) Margin Expansion Reinforces Bullish Narratives Despite P/E Premium
data.description:
Park National (PRK) has rounded out FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of US$140.5 million and basic EPS of US$2.65, alongside trailing twelve month revenue of US$545.7 million and EPS of US$11.18 that sit against an 18.9% earnings increase over the past year. Over the last few quarters, revenue has moved from US$130.6 million in Q4 2024 to US$137.6 million in Q3 2025 and US$140.5 million in Q4 2025, while quarterly EPS has shifted from US$2.39 to US$2.93 and then US$2.65. This sets up a picture where higher trailing net margins and that earnings growth profile are central to how investors read the new results.

33% net margin puts profits in focus

  • Over the last twelve months, Park National generated US$180.1 million of net income on US$545.7 million of revenue, which works out to a 33% net profit margin compared with 29.9% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for the bullish view is that this 18.9% earnings growth over the past year sits alongside a 33% margin and a net interest margin that was between 4.62% and 4.75% in the first three quarters of 2025. This heavily supports the idea of a solid underlying banking franchise but also raises questions about how repeatable this step up is given five year average earnings growth of about 2% a year.
    • Supporters can point to quarterly net income between US$42.2 million and US$48.1 million across 2025 and a cost to income ratio that stayed in a mid 50% to low 60% range as evidence that the bank is keeping a decent handle on costs while converting revenue to profit.
    • On the other hand, critics of the bullish angle may argue that with earnings growth forecasts of about 8.8% a year, the recent 18.9% gain looks like a strong year rather than a long term trend, so they may want to see how margins behave across future reporting periods.

P/E premium and DCF gap pull in different directions

  • Park National is trading on a trailing P/E of 14.4x versus 11.8x for the wider US Banks industry and 12.5x for peers, while the DCF fair value in the data is US$244.02 against the current share price of US$161.10. This suggests the stock is about 34% below that DCF fair value estimate and also sits below an analyst price target of US$180.33.
  • Here, the bullish narrative leans on the combination of that 34% gap to the DCF fair value and the 2.66% dividend yield. Yet the same data also shows the stock carrying a P/E premium, which challenges anyone arguing that the shares look cheap on all measures.
    • Supporters of the bullish case might highlight that trailing EPS of US$11.18, if it holds, means the current P/E of 14.4x is being applied to a business that just posted an 18.9% earnings increase with a 33% net margin, which they may see as consistent with paying somewhat more than the sector average P/E multiple.
    • Skeptics, however, can point out that even though the model based DCF fair value comes in at US$244.02, the forecasts in the data show earnings and revenue growth that are slower than the broader US market, which they may view as a reason to question how much weight to put on that fair value figure compared with the peer and industry P/E levels.

data.title: IonQ’s SkyWater Deal Aims To Build Secure Full Stack Quantum Platform
data.description:
• IonQ (NYSE:IONQ) has agreed to acquire SkyWater Technology in a cash and stock deal valued at about US$1.8b.
  • The transaction will create what IonQ describes as a vertically integrated, full stack quantum computing and semiconductor platform.
  • SkyWater will become a wholly owned subsidiary, keeping its foundry services while adding quantum focused offerings.

For you as an investor, this deal connects IonQ’s quantum computing focus directly with a U.S. based semiconductor foundry, potentially reducing reliance on external chip suppliers. The combined company operates across quantum hardware, networking, security, and sensing, with relevance for government, defense, and aerospace use cases.

IonQ’s move also links quantum development more closely to a domestic manufacturing base, which is an important theme for policymakers and large enterprise buyers. As the integration progresses, investors may track how the company executes on this full stack setup and how it affects development of large scale, fault tolerant quantum processors.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for IonQ by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on IonQ.

Why IonQ could be great value

For IonQ, folding SkyWater into the group directly ties its quantum roadmap to a trusted, U.S. based fabrication base, which could matter a lot for customers in aerospace, defense, and other sensitive sectors. The company is effectively betting that tighter control over chip design, manufacturing, and packaging will help it iterate quantum processors faster and position its federal division as a one stop shop for high security quantum hardware and related semiconductors.

Advertisement

How This Deal Plays Into The IonQ Narrative

IonQ has already been building a story around technical milestones like 99.99% two qubit fidelity, aggressive acquisition activity, and deeper government ties, including the appointment of national security focused executives such as Katie Arrington and an expanded CISO role. Pulling SkyWater into that story extends the theme of building a full quantum ecosystem, from on premise and cloud systems to custom chips and secure infrastructure for government and large enterprise users.

IonQ: Weighing the Rewards and Risks

  • 🎁 Vertical integration with a DMEA accredited U.S. foundry could support long term contracts with government and defense customers that value secure, domestic supply chains.
  • 🎁 The deal may shorten development cycles for large quantum processors, which some investors see as important for IonQ’s competitive position in high fidelity quantum computing.
  • ⚠️ IonQ is still unprofitable and has recently diluted shareholders. Funding a US$1.8b transaction adds execution and capital allocation risk on top of existing losses.
  • ⚠️ Investor sentiment around quantum pure plays has been questioned due to rich valuations and slow commercialization. Expectations around this acquisition could be sensitive to any delays or setbacks.

What To Watch Next

From here, you may want to watch how quickly IonQ and SkyWater align customer pipelines, especially in federal and aerospace, and whether the combined group keeps development and capex within the ranges investors are expecting. data.title: Rocket Lab Fairing Breakthrough Tests Neutron Reusability And Investor Thesis
data.description:
• Rocket Lab has delivered its reusable "Hungry Hippo" captive fairing to its Virginia launch site ahead of the Neutron rocket's first flight.
  • The fairing is designed to stay attached to the rocket throughout the mission to enable rapid reuse and aim for lower launch costs.
  • During recent qualification testing, a Neutron Stage 1 tank ruptured, and the company is progressing with a replacement tank and further tests.

Rocket Lab (NasdaqCM:RKLB) is pushing ahead with Neutron development while introducing hardware that could affect how frequently and at what cost rockets fly. The stock is trading at $87.0, with a return of 23.1% over the past 30 days and 14.5% year to date. Over the past year, the share price has more than tripled, and the 3 year return is around 17 times the starting level.

For investors, the "Hungry Hippo" fairing and the response to the tank rupture provide a view into how Rocket Lab is working to build a reusable launch system for commercial and government customers. Key questions from here include how quickly Neutron moves through testing and how the reusable fairing concept performs once the rocket begins flying.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Rocket Lab by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Rocket Lab.

How Rocket Lab stacks up against its biggest competitors

The arrival of the reusable Hungry Hippo fairing at Wallops Island moves Neutron from design concept to physical hardware that is now entering pre launch testing. For you as an investor, this is important because the fairing is central to Rocket Lab's plan to keep more of the rocket intact between missions, which is aimed at supporting high launch cadence for commercial, civil, and national security customers.

Advertisement

Rocket Lab narrative, put to the test

For investors who follow the long running narrative around Rocket Lab as a small launch provider moving up to heavier payloads, Neutron and Hungry Hippo sit at the core of that story. The company is also preparing to discuss Neutron's schedule and broader progress on its upcoming Q4 2025 earnings call, which may be a key moment for investors tracking how this product fits into views on growth, margins, and capital needs.

Risks and rewards around Neutron and Hungry Hippo

  • Neutron targets payloads up to 13,000 kg, which could open access to larger commercial constellations and higher value national security missions if the rocket reaches regular service.
  • The captive fairing system is designed for rapid reuse, which, if it works as intended, could support more frequent launches from the same hardware set.
  • The recent Stage 1 tank rupture highlights technical risk in Neutron's development, and management has said the schedule impact will only be clear after the test data review.
  • Analysts also flag volatility in the share price over the past 3 months, which can make timing entries and exits more challenging for shorter term holders.

What to watch from here

From here, the key things to watch are the outcome of the current fairing and tank tests, any schedule update on Neutron during the February earnings call, and how customers, including defense agencies, respond as the rocket gets closer to its first launch. If you want to see how other investors are thinking about these developments, you can read community views in this narrative discussion, which can help you weigh the different scenarios for Rocket Lab's product roadmap and risk profile.
